methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $90M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Exowatt, a Series A climate and energy company founded by Hannan Happi and Jack Abraham, has raised $90M to date. The valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (LNZA) operating in the same sector and stage.

Two triangulation methods anchor the analysis. Stage-progression applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier to the $90M capital raised as a proxy baseline, implying a $180M post-money valuation. Peer-median analysis across three same-stage, same-sector comparables—Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M)—yields a median valuation of $150M, with a range of $110M to $200M. These peers are drawn from a broader private frontier-tech cohort including TAE Technologies and Redwood Materials.

The two methods triangulate to a range of $128M (low) to $207M (high), with a median of $165M. This suggests Exowatt should command a valuation in the $150M–$180M band at Series A close. Key uncertainties include relative technology maturity versus named peers, revenue or unit economics data, and the representativeness of the three-company median given sector heterogeneity. The public comp (LNZA) introduces additional valuation reference points but was not weighted equally due to potential stage and business model divergence.
